contributions.heading: My Contributions
contributions.empty: No staged edits yet. Turn on editor mode in Settings, then edit annotations while reading to stage changes here.
contributions.notConnected: Connect your GitHub account on the Contribute page to start staging edits.
contributions.submitHint: This forks the texts repository to your account and opens a pull request for editor review. A merged change goes live only after the corpus is rebuilt.
contributions.appendHint: You already have an open pull request. These edits will be added to it instead of opening a new one.
contributions.reviewAppendNotice: These edits will be added to your open pull request
contributions.errGeneric: Something went wrong while opening the pull request:
contributions.errAppNotInstalled: GitHub refused the write. The Franciscus GitHub App needs to be installed on your account with read & write access to your fork of the texts repository. Open github.com/settings/installations, grant it access (all repositories, or add your franciscus-data fork), then try again.
contributions.errNotAFork: You already have a repository named “franciscus-data” on your account that is not a fork of the project, so a pull request can’t be opened from it. Rename or remove that repository, then try again — the app will create a proper fork.
contributions.errForkFailed: Couldn’t create a fork on your account. If you previously transferred a “franciscus-data” repository to another owner, GitHub keeps a redirect at that name that blocks a fresh fork — create the fork manually from the project’s GitHub page, then try again.

about.title: About
about.body:
Who was Brother Francis really? Eight hundred years after his death, the question still fascinates a great many people — those near to the Catholic faith and those far from it.

Popular accounts often tell of Francis by speaking of his love for poverty as though it were a simple choice of a simpler life, a release from the cares of ownership. The little friar's passion for creation, too, is frequently portrayed as a kind of environmentalism, indebted to its Christian background only by accident of birth or convention.

This reading is often paired with a narrative in which the Church is bent on normalising this controversial figure: first by imposing limits and controls on his charism, and then, after his death, by gradually replacing the authentic testimonies about him with contrived, domesticated, and embellished versions of his story.

Thus would be born Saint Francis — or rather the holy-card Francis: a little shrine, an icon, something that has little or nothing left to do with the poor man Brother Francis.

So who really was this man of Assisi? The friar or the holy card? The answer lies in the primary sources, and in his most precious Testament, and it is unequivocal: neither one nor the other. Between Celano, Bonaventure, the Three Companions, the Testament, and the other sources there are, to be sure, dissonances of interest and purpose: to bear witness to a friend, to recount the birth of a saint, to reflect theologically on his story so as to give a rule to an order that had grown beyond all expectation.

Yet there is no difference of substance. Saint Francis — who was not, but is! — is the man whom all the sources recount in a chorus that is polyphonic but not dissonant: a man radically in love with Christ, and with Christ alone. And it is from this love that everything Francis was descends: a son of the Church, obedient to excess; a dramatic prophet who does not fear to serve a crust of begged bread in the house of a cardinal; a wonderworker; an awestruck lover of Creation — not of mere nature.

This alter Christus, who conformed himself to his Lord even to the point of receiving the stigmata, surely suffers to see his memory divided; suffers, certainly, at pietistic and trivial portrayals, but above all at seeing himself used as a foil to paint the Church as revisionist and censorious.

This is the very reason for this project: to hand you the primary sources — readable, searchable, explorable — in a way that is easy and pleasant.

So that you may come to know, without filters or assumptions, the harmony they recount.

Sancte Francisce, Sancte Bonaventura, orate pro nobis
about.techBody:
This portal is an entirely static site: the texts are stored as plain text files, compiled into a small database that your browser queries directly. No server watches what you read or search, and the search runs entirely on your own device.

The sources are drawn from the standard critical editions, transcribed and marked up according to an open, documented format. The translations and thematic annotations are produced with the help of artificial-intelligence models and are still being reviewed: they are meant as aids to reading and study, not as scholarly editions. The original-source text always remains available for reference.

Everything — the code, the texts, and the content — is offered free of charge, with no advertising and no tracking.

contribute.title: Contribute
contribute.body:
Franciscus is an open project: anyone can report mistakes, propose corrections, or offer new translations.

The project lives in two public repositories on GitHub, a platform where the code and texts are visible to all and where every change is kept on record:

  • the application — the site you are using: github.com/FranciscusApp/franciscus
  • the texts, translations, and annotations: github.com/FranciscusApp/franciscus-data

The two repositories are published under two free licences. The application code is distributed under the AGPL-3.0, which keeps the program free and requires that every modified version remain free in turn, with its source available. The texts, translations, and data are released under CC0 — that is, given over to the public domain, free of any restriction. To contribute is to accept that your work will be distributed under the same licence as the part it belongs to.

You need not be a technical expert. If you know how to use Git, you can open an issue or propose a change directly (a pull request). If you would rather not — and especially if you are a scholar wishing to offer verified translations, not machine-produced ones — it is enough to write to info@verbumcaro.it, clearly identifying the passages you have translated and including an explicit declaration releasing them under CC0.
contribute.githubBody: Connect your own GitHub account to propose changes directly from the app. Your edits are gathered into a pull request on the texts repository and reviewed by an editor before they are merged. A merged change becomes visible only after the corpus is rebuilt.
contribute.consentLabel: I understand that all contributions to the texts, translations, and annotations are released under CC0, placing them in the public domain.
contribute.githubNoScript: Enable JavaScript to connect your GitHub account.
contribute.errPopupBlocked: The sign-in window was blocked. Please allow pop-ups for this site and try again.
contribute.errPopupClosed: The sign-in window was closed before finishing.
contribute.connectBody: Development is organized on the VerbumCaro Discord server — come by to follow the work, ask questions, or lend a hand. You can also visit verbumcaro.it or write to info@verbumcaro.it.
